source.title: BSEE Accident Investigation Report - Allision during Pipeline Decommissioning

event.top_event: Allision - Motor Vessel collision with Tension Leg Platform
actions_taken: Hard handrails installed to prevent future access to damaged area
Q4000 semisubmersible hired to complete pipeline removal
Motor Vessel moved 500 meters away from facility
Onsite investigation conducted by BSEE on 14 February 2020
event.summary: On 02 February 2020 at 1230 hours, a Motor Vessel engaged in pipeline riser disconnection and abandonment operations at the Ewing Banks 921-A tension leg platform experienced an allision with the platform. The vessel's 300-ton winch became overwhelmed by the pipeline weight, the shackle wedged into the TLP sheave, and despite 100% power application, the vessel was pulled toward the platform resulting in collision. A hold-back cable severed, increasing reverse tension on the deck winch cable. Total damage: $336,000 (TLP: $161K, M/V: $175K).
key_phrases: engineering calculations not confirmed against vessel capacity
forward thrust applied to assist hoisting

hazards.name: Heavy pipeline load during disconnection and abandonment
Vessel positioning and dynamic positioning failure
Rigging and cable failure under load

threats.name: Vessel bollard pull capacity exceeded during lift operations
Shackle wedging in TLP sheave causing load lock
Hold-back cable severing under reverse tension
Vessel unable to maintain position despite maximum thruster power

consequences.name: Structural damage to TLP top deck, handrails, and piping

controls.name: Job Safety Analysis (JSA) meeting prior to rigging operations
controls.barrier_role: Administrative control to identify hazards and plan safe work

supporting_text: A job safety analysis (JSA) meeting was conducted before rigging up a 12-inch pipeline.
The crew noted weather in favorable conditions with clear skies, 5-7-foot seas and 15 knot winds.

controls.name: Written procedures for pipeline disconnection operations
controls.barrier_role: Administrative control to ensure standardized safe work practices

supporting_text: The crew began following the written procedures. Operations were proceeding fine according to the crews.

controls.name: Vessel bollard pull capacity verification and engineering calculations
controls.barrier_role: Engineering control to ensure vessel has adequate capacity for the lift
controls.lod_basis: Pre-operation engineering assessment and vessel specification confirmation

supporting_text: Before the lift, the engineering and installation contractor reported to ENI that the vessel they chartered to conduct the work on ENI's behalf had sufficient capacity (bollard pull) to hold position while the pipeline was being disconnected and abandoned. It was discovered this was incorrect while trying to remove the disconnected riser.
The engineering and installation contractor performed extensive calculations for all stages of the lift, but they did not confirm the vessel's bollard pull against these calculations.
Vessel thrusters were performing as designed according to the vessel owner. However, the vessel was not designed for the required bollard pull.

controls.name: Motor Vessel thruster system and dynamic positioning capability
controls.barrier_role: Engineering control to maintain vessel position during lift operations
controls.lod_basis: Vessel propulsion and positioning system design

supporting_text: Suddenly, the weight of the pipeline overwhelmed the power of the M/V crane. The main 300-ton winch located on the M/V was paying out unexpectedly.
With the momentum the pipeline descending, the M/V was pulling toward the TLP despite the using 100% power and could not be stopped in time.
Vessel thrusters were performing as designed according to the vessel owner. However, the vessel was not designed for the required bollard pull.

controls.name: Hold-back cable system to prevent reverse tension on vessel
controls.barrier_role: Engineering control to prevent vessel pull-in during load reversal

supporting_text: A hold-back cable (that was attached from the platform to the riser) severed, which resulted in additional reverse tension being applied to the vessel's deck winch cable.
The sudden increase in reverse tension on the deck winch cable pulled the M/V toward the Platform.

controls.name: Shackle and sheave design to prevent wedging during load transfer
controls.barrier_role: Engineering control to prevent load lock during rigging operations
controls.lod_basis: Mechanical rigging equipment design and compatibility

supporting_text: The shackle attached to the main line became wedged into the sheave on the TLP.
After several attempts to free the shackle, the M/V was unsuccessful in freeing the wedged shackle.

controls.name: Operator command and control procedures for vessel maneuvering during lifts
controls.barrier_role: Administrative control to ensure proper coordination between deck operations and vessel control

supporting_text: At 1245 hours, the Captain, in command at the aft DP control station, applied forward thrust (at the request of the onboard project supervisor) in an attempt to assist the deck winch in hoisting the riser.

controls.name: Emergency response and incident containment procedures
controls.barrier_role: Administrative control to limit consequences after collision occurs
controls.lod_basis: Post-incident response and facility safety measures

supporting_text: In an effort to make the damaged area safe, hard handrails have been installed to prevent any future access.
After the M/V was unsuccessful in removing the pipeline, ENI hired the Q4000 semisubmersible to complete the job.

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
